# ✨ AppFunctions

AppFunctions allow your Android app to share specific pieces of functionality that the system and various AI agents and assistants can discover and invoke. By defining these functions, you enable your app to provide services, data, and actions to the Android OS, allowing users to complete tasks through AI agents and system-level interactions.  AppFunctions serve as the mobile equivalent of tools within the Model Context Protocol (MCP). While MCP traditionally standardizes how agents connect to server-side tools, AppFunctions provide the same mechanism for Android apps. This enables you to expose your app's capabilities as orchestratable "tools" that authorized apps (callers) can discover and execute to fulfill user intents. Callers must have the EXECUTE_APP_FUNCTIONS permission to discover and execute AppFunctions, and can include agents, apps, and AI assistants like Gemini. Read more about it [here](https://developer.android.com/ai/appfunctions)

**They work with devices running Android 16 or higher.**

In NotyKT, it was implemented in [this PR](https://github.com/PatilShreyas/NotyKT/pull/823/) when the latest version of appfunctions package was `1.0.0-alpha08`. _So if you're reading this far later, lot of things might have been changed 🤷🏻_.

## How it works?

In this ecosystem, **NotyKT** acts as the **Tool App**. It registers its capabilities with the system. A separate app acts as the **Agent**, orchestrating the user's intent.

Here is how the execution flow works under the hood:

<svg version="1.1" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 1422.8849925994873 720" height="500"><!-- svg-source:excalidraw --><metadata></metadata><defs><style class="style-fonts">

dominant-baseline="alphabetic">Display Notes in Chat</text><text x="84.75992584228516" y="34.096000000000004" font-family="Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji" font-size="16px" fill="#1e1e1e" text-anchor="middle" style="white-space: pre;" direction="ltr" dominant-baseline="alphabetic">UI</text></g></svg>

## Executing AppFunctions

First of all, install the application on the device. There are two ways to test the app:

### 💻 ADB shell commands (Recommended)

#### 1. List functions

Following command lists all the app functions available:
```shell
# Lists all the app functions exposed by all apps installed on the device.
adb shell cmd app_function list-app-functions
# Package specific
adb shell cmd app_function list-app-functions --package dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp
```

The output could look as follows

```json
{
  "dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp": [
    {
      "AppFunctionStaticMetadata-dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp": {
        "serviceName": ["androidx.appfunctions.service.PlatformAppFunctionService"],
        "description": ["Lists all available notes."],
        "response": [
          {
            "valueType": [
              {
                "type": [10],
                "itemType": [
                  {
                    "dataTypeReference": ["dev.shreyaspatil.noty.appfunctions.Note"],
                    "type": [11],
                    "isNullable": [false]
                  }
                ],
                "isNullable": [false]
              }
            ]
          }
        ],
        "enabledByDefault": [true],
        "functionId": ["dev.shreyaspatil.noty.appfunctions.NotyAppFunctions#listNotes"],
        "packageNameHash": [-678864315],
        "scope": ["global"],
        "mobileApplicationQualifiedId": ["android$apps-db\/apps#dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp"],
        "packageName": ["dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp"]
      },
      // ... Other app functions
    },
    // ... Other package\'s functions
  ]
}
```

### Execute the functions

The adb shell command `adb shell cmd app_function execute-app-function` should be used to execute the app function.

### Example 1: Listing the notes.

```shell
adb shell cmd app_function execute-app-function --package dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp --function dev.shreyaspatil.noty.appfunctions.NotyAppFunctions#listNotes --parameters {}
```

Output:

```shell
{
  "androidAppfunctionsReturnValue": [
    {
      "id": ["TMP-053ffdbe-7997-4ba6-9187-3a4ef6be0833"],
      "title": ["💡Ideas"],
      "content": ["- Android AppFunctions demo\n- Android Foldable device APIs\n- Generative AI"]
    },
    {
      "id": ["TMP-58832e3e-da0f-4542-aa55-68fbc9edea19"],
      "title": ["👨🏻‍💻 Today's tasks"],
      "content": ["Work on the open-source sample to demonstrate the use of Android AppFunctions"]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Example 2: Adding a note

Run the following command with parameters for creation of a note

```shell
adb shell "cmd app_function execute-app-function \
  --package dev.shreyaspatil.noty.composeapp \
  --function dev.shreyaspatil.noty.appfunctions.NotyAppFunctions#createNote \
  --parameters '{\"title\":\"hello from shell\",\"content\":\"created from the terminal\"}'"
```

It'll show the output of a function returned value and then the note will be added in the app.

### 🤖 Sample Agent App

The AppFunctions can be executed from the app which can query other apps in the device and execute
those functions. **But only system apps are allowed to do that.** So apps like Gemini in the device
could be able to invoke the app functions declared by the application. 

The [sample agent app is created here](https://github.com/PatilShreyas/appfunctions-notyagent-app) just to experience the agentic functionality. Follow the steps
described there to run and execute the app.

Sample agent looks like this:

![](https://github.com/PatilShreyas/appfunctions-notyagent-app/raw/refs/heads/main/demo.gif)
